segments.s058-g113: This is a compact nominal phrase + verb: 'دل زال' (Zal's heart) is a genitive construction (possessor after possessed). 'یکباره' is an adverb ('suddenly/at once') that precedes the verb. 'دیوانه گشت' is a past-tense/perfective verbal phrase where گشت (here 'became') is the finite verb and دیوانه is the predicate adjective. The order is possessee + possessor, adverb, predicate — a typical Persian poetic order.
segments.s058-g114: Two short clauses joined without an explicit conjunction. 'خرد دور شد' literally 'wisdom became distant/was gone' — شد is the past/preterite of شدن used as 'became' or 'went.' 'عشق فرزانه گشت' parallels the first clause: عشق (subject) + فرزانه (predicate adjective 'wise/sagacious') + گشت (past of گشت 'became'). The verb گشت is used like شدن to mark a change of state.
segments.s058-g115: A stacked noun phrase in apposition: 'سپهدار' (commander/title) is the head noun, modified by the adjective/national epithet 'تازی' (Arab/Arabian). 'سر راستان' is an epithet in apposition (literally 'upright head' or 'chief of the righteous'); Persian poetic style often places epithets after the main title. No finite verb appears here because the verb comes in the following segment.
segments.s058-g116: 'بگوید' is the finite verb meaning 'he tells/relates' but the supplied sense list does not include the verb sense (see senses mapping). 'بر این' is a prepositional phrase 'about this' or 'concerning this' (بر + demonstrative). The second 'بر' + 'یکی داستان' functions like 'of one tale' or 'a certain tale' — here بر + یکی + داستان = 'a certain story about this.' Word order is verb + prepositional complements; in poetry the verb may precede or follow its objects for metrical reasons.
segments.s058-g117: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause. تا زنده‌ام is a temporal clause formed with تا + the present/enclitic verb زنده‌ام (= "as long as I am alive"). چرمه (a leathern coat/garment) is the noun object/topic and جفت منست is a verbless predicate: جفت ("pair/companion") + منست (an archaic copular/adjectival word meaning "is fitting/appropriate"). The whole clause states a continuing condition and its fitting accompaniment.
segments.s058-g118: خم چرخ گردان is a nominal phrase in which خم ("bend/curve") is specified by چرخ گردان ("the turning/revolving wheel" — i.e., fortune/the wheel of fate). نهفت is the past participle/adjectival verb meaning "hidden/concealed" and functions as the predicate; منست again follows to mark that this hidden state is fitting/appropriate. The object of نهفت is implicit (what was listed earlier), so the clause says the wheel's bend has kept them hidden, which is regarded as proper.
segments.s058-g119: عروسم (my bride) appears as the topical/possessive noun. نباید is the impersonal negative modal meaning "one should not / must not." The clause که رعنا شوم is a subordinate result/avoidance clause: که + رعنا (adjective "graceful/beautiful") + شوم (1st-person present subjunctive/potential "I become"). Together the phrase expresses prohibition to avoid the stated result.
segments.s058-g120: به نزد is a prepositional phrase meaning "in the presence of / before." خردمند is the noun "the wise (person)" and رسوا is the predicative adjective "disgraced/exposed." شوم is again the 1st-person potential/subjunctive "I become" expressing the feared outcome. Grammatically this completes the avoidance clause introduced earlier (i.e., 'lest I become disgraced before the wise').
segments.s058-g57: دو چشمش is the possessed noun phrase 'her two eyes' (چشم + enclitic ـش = 'her eye(s)'). به سان is a fixed simile marker meaning 'like/as' (به + سان). دو نرگس به باغ literally 'two narcissi of/in the garden' — به باغ attaches to نرگس to locate them in the garden.
segments.s058-g58: مژه تیرگی برده is a verbless (or participial) clause where مژه (eyelashes) is the subject and برده is the past participle of بردن used as a perfect/periphrastic predicate meaning 'has taken' or 'has acquired.' از introduces the source ('from a crow's feather'), and پر زاغ is a genitive construction 'feather of the crow.'
segments.s058-g59: دو ابرو 'her two eyebrows' is the subject. به سان introduces a simile 'like/as.' کمان طراز is an adjectival phrase: کمان 'a bow' and طراز 'fashioned/made in a style' — together 'a well‑shaped (or artistically made) bow.'
segments.s058-g60: بر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon her' (or 'on it/them,' here referring to the eyebrows/face). توز is an archaic/obscure noun placed as the thing laid/on display; پوشیده is a past participle 'covered/laid over.' از marks the material/quality 'with/from' (here 'scented with'), followed by مشک 'musk' and ناز functioning as a quality noun 'coquetry/dainty charm.' Grammatically, پوشیده governs the following از-phrase to specify what it is covered/imbued with.
segments.s058-g61: بهشتیست is an adjectival/predicative form 'is paradisal/heavenly' (the -است copula is contained in the form). سرتاسر آراسته is an intensive predicate 'adorned all over/throughout,' with سرتاسر ('throughout') modifying آراسته ('adorned').
segments.s058-g62: پر here is an adjectival use meaning 'full (of)'; it governs a coordinate noun phrase of qualities: آرایش 'adornment', رامش 'tranquility/solace', و خواسته 'and desire/wish.' The list is what she is 'full of.'
segments.s058-g63: برآورد is a past verb meaning 'caused/put into' (here 'caused to boil/rise'); it takes مر ('for me'/'on my account') and the direct object زال را ('Zal' marked by را). دل به جوش is the resultant state 'the heart into a boil/surge' — جوش used figuratively as 'ardor/passion.'
segments.s058-g64: چنان شد is an idiom 'it became so' / 'it came to be such.' کزو is a relative/adverbial pronoun 'from which' referring back to دل; رفت here means 'went' in the sense 'departed/vanished.' آرام وهوش are the items that left — آرام 'calm' and وهوش 'and (the) sense/awareness' — so the clause says his calm and wits departed from him.
segments.s058-g9: This is a coordinated adjectival/participial phrase describing the (implicit) subject. گشاده (adjective) + در گنج (prepositional phrase) = “generous/open in treasure” (adjective before its complement). The second coordinated part, افگنده رنج, uses the past participle افگنده (from افکندن) meaning “having cast/laid” with رنج as its direct object; both conjuncts jointly qualify the same subject.
segments.s058-g10: بر is the preposition “upon/on”; آیین و رسم are two coordinated nouns (‘rite/ritual’ and ‘custom’) that form the object of بر. سرای here is a noun ‘house/place’ governing سپنج (a proper name or specific place); together they mean ‘the house/place called Sepanj’ — the supplied senses list doesn’t include this exact noun sense, so the lemma senses for سرای and سپنج don’t match directly.
segments.s058-g11: A straightforward verbal clause: ز (poetic 'from') marks origin (ز زابل = 'from Zābul'), به کابل is the goal ('to Kabul'), رسید is the past-tense verb 'arrived/reached'. آن زمان is an adverbial phrase 'that time / at that time' placed after the verb for emphasis. The implied subject is understood from context (third person).
segments.s058-g12: A nominal phrase listing characteristics or companions: گرازان (plural noun) + و + خندان (adjective/participle 'laughing/cheerful') + و + دل شادمان (literally 'heart joyful' — a verbless predicate meaning 'hearts/people were joyful'). The coordination ties these qualities together as describing the scene or the people present.
segments.s058-g13: یکی is the indefinite ‘a certain’ (introducing someone), پادشا is the noun 'king', and بود is the past copula 'was'. مهراب نام is the name phrase 'named Mehrāb' (نام after the proper name marks 'called/named'). The clause states existence/identity in past tense.
segments.s058-g14: زبر دست is an adjectival compound meaning 'mighty/strong in hand/power' (زبر + دست); با گنج uses the preposition با 'with' to indicate possession of treasure; و links to گسترده کام where گسترده (adjective) modifies کام (noun 'satisfaction'/'fulfillment'), i.e. 'with treasure and wide/ample satisfaction'. The whole phrase further describes that king.
segments.s058-g15: به بالا is an adverbial phrase 'on high / lofty'; به کردار = 'in manner/conduct' (کردار as 'behavior'), آزاده is an adjectival epithet 'noble/free-spirited', and سرو is a simile/metaphor 'cypress' (used to liken his bearing to a tall, noble cypress). Word order places adverbials before the noun image.
segments.s058-g16: به رخ 'in face/appearance' + چون بهار 'like the spring' is a clear simile for his countenance. و connects to به رفتن تذرو, where به رفتن literally 'in going / in his walk' and تذرو (a present/poetic form of 'to scatter') describes what happens when he walks: 'he scatters/disperses' (i.e. causes things to scatter or spreads charm/effects).
segments.s058-g1: چنان بد که روزی چنان کرد رای is an adjectival + result clause. چنان بد (literally “so/bad”) is an emphatic adjective phrase meaning “so ill/bad.” که introduces the result/that-clause; روزی functions adverbially as “one day/once” (classical usage), and چنان کرد رای is a past action: کرد (past “did/made”) + رای (the noun “counsel/plan”) meaning “he made such a plan/decision.”
segments.s058-g2: که در پادشاهی بجنبد ز جای continues the result clause introduced by که. در پادشاهی is a prepositional phrase “in/at the kingship/throne”; بجنبد is a present/subjunctive‑style verbal form meaning “will move/be stirred/be roused” (classical/poetic mood indicating the possible/resultant motion); ز جای is a postpositional phrase “from its place.”
segments.s058-g3: برون‌رفت با ویژه‌گردان خویش uses برون‌رفت (a noun/nominal verbal form ‘exit/going out’ used here as a verbal action) followed by the comitative phrase با ویژه‌گردان خویش “with his elite corps.” خویش is expressed as خوی + enclitic ش (possessive), so the possession is attached as an enclitic pronoun.
segments.s058-g4: که با او یکی بودشان رای و کیش is a relative/result clause introduced by که: با او “with him” indicates relationship or alignment; یکی بودشان (literally “they were one”) says their view/agreement coincided; رای و کیش are two nouns in apposition meaning “opinion/counsel and creed/sect.” بودشان is the past copula with enclitic pronoun marking the possessor/subject.
segments.s058-g5: سوی کشور هندوان کرد رای places the destination first: سوی کشور هندوان “toward the country of the Hindus.” کرد رای is the past action “he made (this) counsel/decision” — common classical word order where the decision (رای) is named after کرد.
segments.s058-g6: سوی کابل و دنبر و مرغ و مای is a simple coordinated list of destinations introduced again by سوی. Each place is joined by و (and); the whole segment functions as the target of the decision in the preceding clause.
segments.s058-g7: به هر جایگاهی بیاراستی is a prepositional phrase + verb. به هر جایگاهی “to/for every station/place” sets the domain; بیاراستی is a classical poetic past verb meaning “he arranged/ornamented/placed (it)” — here expressing that he made preparations or adorned every post/place.
segments.s058-g8: می‌و رود و رامشگران خواستی lists what he supplied: می و رود = “wine and (string) instrument/music” (می = wine, رود here denotes a musical instrument or music in context), رامشگران is a plural noun “comforters/entertainers/peacemakers,” and خواستی is the past verb “he wanted/desired,” so the sequence means he provided wine, music, and entertainers according to his wish.
segments.s058-g121: از اندیشگان is a prepositional/cause phrase placed before the subject to indicate cause or source ('because of/the wise men'). زال is the subject. شد is the past copular/verb 'became' (classical past). خسته‌دل is a compound predicate/adjective meaning 'one with a weary heart' describing Zal. The order (cause phrase + subject + شد + predicate) is a poetic inversion but follows classical Persian usage.
segments.s058-g122: بر آن کار is a prepositional phrase 'upon that affair/with regard to that matter' and is fronted. بنهاد is the simple past 'he placed/settled' (here: 'he set'). پیوسته is an adverb 'constantly/continually' and modifies the action; دل ('heart') is the direct object meaning 'he fixed/placed his heart (on it)'. The word order (prepositional phrase + verb + adverb + object) reflects a poetic inversion — in prose one would expect دل پیوسته بنهاد یا دل را پیوسته بنهاد.
segments.s058-g123: همی بود is a classical continuous/past habitual construction ('was ever/kept on'). پیچان is a participial/adjectival form 'twisting/writhing' that modifies دل ('heart'); دل is the theme/experiencer. از گفت‌وگوی gives the cause/trigger ('from because of the conversation' = 'by the talk'). The clause therefore means the heart was continually agitated by the talk.
segments.s058-g124: مگر here introduces a clause of fear/negative purpose roughly 'lest/for fear that'. تیره is an adjectival predicate 'dark/tarnished'. گردد is the present/optative of گشت used after مگر to express potentiality ('may become / might be'). از این آبروی is a prepositional/relational phrase 'of/with this honour/reputation' (the thing that could become tarnished). Together: 'lest this honour be darkened.'
segments.s058-g125: همی گشت is another iterative/past-habitual expression 'would turn/kept turning'. یک چند means 'for a short while / for some time' and limits the duration. بر سر سپهر is a poetic locative phrase 'upon/toward the summit/vault of the sky' meaning 'toward the heavens' or 'to the sky' — the phrase indicates the direction of the turning (gaze or thought).
segments.s058-g126: دل زال is a genitive/possessive sequence 'the heart of Zal' (note classical order: head noun before possessor). آگنده is a predicate adjective 'filled' and یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/wholly' modifying it. به مهر ('with love') uses به + noun to indicate content/means ('filled with love'). The whole segment: 'Zal's heart was entirely filled with love.'
segments.s058-g73: برون‌رفت is a past action 'came out / went out' (verb phrase) placed before the subject — a common poetic inversion. مهراب کابل خدای is a name with an epithet: مهراب (the proper name) followed by کابل خدای, literally 'Kabul‑lord' (خدای used as a title 'lord of Kabul'). The phrase functions as the subject of the verb.
segments.s058-g74: سوی (toward) marks motion in the direction of خیمه (tent/pavilion). زال زابل خدای is another name + title construction: زال (the hero) qualified by زابل خدای 'lord of Zābul'. The whole segment expresses movement toward Zāl's tent and repeats the honorific pattern.
segments.s058-g75: چو آمد = 'when he came' (چو used as a temporal subordinating particle 'when'); به نزدیکی = 'to the vicinity / near'; بارگاه = 'the royal court' (the noun completes the locative phrase). Word order is subject‑verb with the temporal clause fronted.
segments.s058-g76: خروش آمد = 'a cry/roar arose' (noun + آمد 'came/rose'). از در = 'from the gate/door' (از marks source; در here means 'gate'). که introduces the content clause or reported speech that follows; بگشای is an imperative/optative form 'open' (ب‑ is the classical preverb, here producing the present/imperative flavor) and راه is the object 'the way/path'. The line reports an exclamation commanded at the gate.
segments.s058-g77: بر + پهلوان expresses motion 'toward the hero' (بر here = toward/to). اندرون رفت is the past 'entered inside' (an intransitive motion verb). گو after the verb is a classical particle meaning 'saying' or 'so he said' — it links the entrance to reported speech or manner.
segments.s058-g78: به سان = 'like / in the manner of' (به + سان); درختی is an indefinite 'a tree' (‑ی suffix), پر از = 'full of', بار نو = 'new/fresh fruit/load' (بار here = produce/load). The whole phrase is a simile describing his appearance or bearing on entering.
segments.s058-g79: دل زال شد شاد = 'Zāl's heart became glad' (possessive phrase دل زال 'Zāl's heart' + شد 'became' + شاد 'glad'). و connects this clause to بنواختش, a past action: بنواختش is the preterite 'he struck/played it' with enclitic ‑ش referring to an instrument or the action's object; in poetic context it means 'he played (music)'.
segments.s058-g80: از آن انجمن = 'from that assembly' (از marking source, آن = 'that', انجمن = 'assembly'). سر برافراختش is a compound past: سر برافراخت 'he raised his head' with the enclitic ‑ش echoing the object/affected item; برافراخت is the causative/past 'lifted/raised up'. The segment describes the proud or joyful gesture following the music/joy.
segments.s058-g41: خوش‌آمد is an impersonal/past expression 'it pleased' (literally 'pleasant came'); its logical subject is دیدار او ('the sight/meeting of her'). هماناش (here written together) intensifies/demonstrates 'that very', placed before the subject for poetic emphasis; او is the referent 'her' (the one seen). Word order is emphatic: intensifier + predicate + subject.
segments.s058-g42: دلش = دل + possessive ـش 'his heart/feeling'. تیز‌تر گشت is a comparative predicate: تیز‌تر 'keener/more eager' + گشت 'became' (past of گشتن = become). در کار او is a prepositional phrase 'in/with regard to his affair/matter concerning her' that completes the thought; the verb گشت governs the resulting state.
segments.s058-g43: چو here = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. مهراب is the proper name Mehrab (not the architectural mihrab); برخاست is past 'rose/stood up' and از خوان 'from the banquet/table' gives the source. Word order is standard temporal clause + subject + verb + adjunct.
segments.s058-g44: نگه کرد is a light-periphrastic past 'made/kept a gaze' = 'looked/gazed'. زال is the subject performing the action. اندر آن برز و یال is a prepositional phrase 'upon that high/brilliant crest and mane' (اندر = in/on; آن = that; برز و یال = lofty/exalted aspect and mane), placed after the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.s058-g45: چنین گفت = 'thus he said'; با مهتران 'to/with the chiefs/elders' indicates the addressees. زال is the speaker; زر begins the content/topic of what he utters ('gold'). In classical Persian the reporting verb precedes the addressee phrase and the quoted topic can follow directly.
segments.s058-g46: که introduces the substantive/subordinate clause 'that ...'. زیبنده‌تر is the comparative 'more fitting/befitting'. زین here functions as 'the saddle' (زین = saddle/harness) rather than a demonstrative. The second که introduces the infinitival/subjunctive action بندد (present-subjunctive form used after که) 'to bind/fasten', with کمر 'belt/waist' as its object: 'more fitting than a saddle that (one) should bind the belt with.'
segments.s058-g47: یکی نامدار = 'a certain renowned (man)'; یکی is the indefinite 'a certain', نامدار 'famous/renowned'. از میان مهان = 'from among the mehān/guests/nobles' (از میان = from among). This is the identifying preface to the reported speech.
segments.s058-g48: چنین گفت = 'thus (he) said' introduces the quotation. کای is the vocative particle 'O', addressing پهلوان 'champion/hero'; جهان 'of the world' qualifies the epithet (hero of the world). The vocative appears directly before the addressed title.
segments.s058-g25: The phrase is a short verbless clause in which the past-participle/adjective آراسته ('adorned') comes after the things it qualifies (گنج و اسپان). Classical/poetic Persian often places the adjective after its nouns; the initial ابا here functions as an enclitic/archaic particle (not the usual با 'with') and is best read as a prepositional marker introducing the means ('with' / 'by') — the exact form is archaic/poetic.
segments.s058-g26: غلامان and the following phrase are coordinated (غلامان و هر گونه‌ای). خواسته is a past-participle/adjectival form meaning 'desired' or 'sought' and modifies the preceding group; هر گونه‌ای ('every/any kind') is an indefinite quantifier working as the direct domain of خواسته.
segments.s058-g27: This is a poetic noun-list introduced by the preposition ز (here meaning "from/with"). The coordinating و links a string of nouns (دینار، یاقوت، مشک، عبیر) in the usual right-to-left order; no verb appears — it is a series of items or possessions. Each noun stands alone (not inflected) and the initial ز applies to the whole list.
segments.s058-g28: Another coordinated noun-phrase also introduced by ز. دیبای (a type of brocaded silk) is qualified by زربفت ("gold-woven"), while چینی ("Chinese") modifies حریر ("silk"). The modifier can follow (زربفت after دیبای) or precede (چینی before حریر) depending on idiomatic/poetic word order.
segments.s058-g29: یکی is the indefinite/numeral 'one' introducing a single item. تاج is the headword 'crown' and با + گوهر gives the instrument/ornament ('a crown with a gem'); شاهوار is a postpositive adjective meaning 'regal/lofty' qualifying گوهر (the adjective follows the noun).
segments.s058-g30: Again one item introduced by یکی. طوق زرین is a noun + adjective phrase ('a golden collar/necklace'), and زبرجد نگار is a compound epithet: زبرجد names the gem (peridot) and نگار functions as an ornamental epithet ('adorned/ornamented with'); in poetry such epithets often follow the noun they modify.
segments.s058-g31: چو آمد is a common poetic temporal clause meaning 'when (it) came'; the motion verb آمد ('came') takes a locative phrase به دستان سام ('to Sam's hands'), an idiom meaning 'reached Sam' or 'Sam received'. آگهی ('news/information') is the thing that 'came' — news came to Sam.
segments.s058-g32: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that ...'. مهراب is a personal name (here the agent of آمد). آمد is the past verb 'came'. The sequence ب + دین is written here as separate items but functions as the prepositional phrase 'بدین' = 'to this'; فرهی is an adjective/noun meaning 'noble' or 'of noble rank' and refers to the destination/context. The clause therefore states that Mahrab 'came to this noble [place/person]'.
segments.s058-g105: چو/بر-phrase: 'بر او' is a prepositional phrase marking the target of the gaze ('upon/at him') placed before the rest of the clause for emphasis. 'هیچکس' is the subject 'no one' and 'چشم' functions as a verbal complement meaning 'a look/gaze'; the verb 'نگماشتند' is past plural (third person) and here is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'looked/cast (a glance)'. The whole clause therefore reads literally 'upon him no one a gaze looked.'
segments.s058-g106: 'او را' is the definite object phrase ('him') marked by the object particle 'را'. 'ز' here is the poetic 'of/from' introducing the class he is counted among ('of the madmen'). 'داشتند' is past plural 'they held/considered' (literally 'they had [him] as ...'), so the clause means 'they considered him from/of the madmen.' The initial word 'مر' is an archaic/enclitic element (uncertain function in classical usage) and does not alter the main syntactic relation.
segments.s058-g107: The clause begins with the temporal conjunction 'چو' = 'when'. 'روشن‌دل' is an epithet (adjective) modifying 'پهلوان' ('the clear‑hearted/bright‑souled champion'). 'پهلوان را' is marked with the object particle 'را' (the hero is the referent perceived). 'بدوی' is an archaic/poetic form here (uncertain in the supplied senses) that links this noun phrase into the ensuing perceptual clause; the perception itself is completed by the verb 'دیدند' in the next segment.
segments.s058-g108: 'چنان گرم' is an adverbial phrase 'so/so very fervent' modifying the perception. 'دیدند' is past plural 'they saw/found'. 'با گفت‌وگوی' uses 'با' ('with/by') to indicate manner — 'in/through conversation' — and 'گفت‌وگوی' is a genitive/construct form 'conversation'. Together: 'they saw him so fervent in conversation.'
segments.s058-g109: Again the phrase 'مر او را' introduces the object 'him' (see previous note about the archaic 'مر'). 'ستودند' is past plural 'they praised/admired'. The agent is expressed after the verb: 'یک‌یک مهان' = 'the guests one by one' (word order where the agent phrase follows the verb for emphasis).
segments.s058-g110: 'همان' here = 'the very one' / 'that one'; 'کز' is the relative 'که از/که ز' = 'who/which (from/of)'. 'پس پرده' is a compound meaning 'behind the veil/curtain' ('پس' = 'after/behind' + 'پرده' = 'curtain'). 'بودش' is past 'was' with enclitic pronoun '-ش' referring back to 'همان', and 'نهان' is the predicate adjective 'hidden.' The relative clause therefore: 'the very one who was hidden behind the curtain.'
segments.s058-g111: This is a nominative/causal listing introduced by the preposition 'ز' (poetic 'from/because of') and joined by 'و' ('and'). Each noun names a quality: 'بالا' = 'high/lofty' (here 'loftiness, high station'), 'دیدار' = 'appearance/meeting' (here likely 'his bearing/appearance' or 'the sight of him'), and 'آهستگی' = 'gentleness/softness.' The series functions adverbially to give reasons.
segments.s058-g112: Parallel continuation: 'ز' again = 'because of/from', 'بایستگی' (here best read as 'fitness/appropriateness' or 'necessity/what is proper') and 'شایستگی' = 'worthiness/merit'. 'هم' = 'also/too', linking this pair to the previous list and reinforcing that these qualities are further reasons.
segments.s058-g97: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' placed before the verb. بشنید is a past tense verb 'he heard' (3rd singular). مهراب is the subject (proper name); کرد آفرین is a light verb construction where کرد (did/made) takes آفرین (an acclamation) as its object, meaning 'he uttered/cried "Afarin."
segments.s058-g98: This clause is somewhat inverted/poetic: به دل 'into the heart' (the directional sense of به) comes first, then the definite object marker را follows Zal (زال را) marking him as the object, and خواند (past of خواندن) is the verb 'he recited/caused to be implanted.' ناپاک دین is the thing acted on ('impure religion'). Together the phrase says he put/recited the impure religion into Zal's heart.
segments.s058-g99: خرامان is an adverb/adverbial participle meaning 'with a proud/stately gait' modifying the action. برفت is a past tense verbal form 'he went/left' (poetic spelling), and از بر تخت اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from upon his throne' (از ... بر = 'from off of', تخت اوی = 'his throne' with اوی as the possessive).
segments.s058-g100: همی is an intensifying/adverbial particle here meaning 'continually' or 'kept on.' آفرین خواند uses the same light-verb pattern as before: خواند (past) with آفرین as its object, 'he uttered/cried "Afarin".' بر بخت اوی is a prepositional phrase 'over/upon his fortune' (بر = 'on/upon', بخت = 'fate/fortune', اوی = 'his').
segments.s058-g101: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' دستان سام is a noun phrase ('Sam's hands' literally; in poetry this can refer to Sam's people/retainers) that functions as the subject. از پسش ('from/behind him') gives the viewpoint/direction and بنگرید is the past subjunctive/poetic past 'they looked/gazed' (here rendered as a past action 'looked').
segments.s058-g102: ستودش is the past verb 'praised him' with the object clitic -ش. فراوان is an adverb 'abundantly/much.' چنان چون سزید is an idiomatic short clause 'just as he deserved' (چنان = 'so/thus', چون = 'as', سزید = past of 'to deserve' or 'to be fitting'), qualifying the manner of the praise.
segments.s058-g103: از آن کو is an archaic/poetic relative phrase 'from that one who' (کو is an older relative/ interrogative form). نه هم ... و هم ... بود is a negative correlational construction meaning 'there was neither ... nor ...' (here دین 'religion' and راه 'way' are the two terms). The clause states that for that person neither religion nor way applied.
segments.s058-g104: زبان is 'the tongue' metonymically meaning 'speech/words.' از here carries a causal/limiting nuance 'from/because of' (i.e., 'for praising him'), ستودنش is a nominalized verb 'praising him' (ستودن + object clitic -ش), کوتاه بود is the predicate 'was short/was inadequate.' The whole means words were inadequate to praise him.
segments.s058-g49: پس is a narrative particle here meaning “then,” introducing what follows. پرده او is a noun + possessive phrase (“her veil”) with the pronoun او after پرده instead of an ezāfe; یکی دخترست is an inverted predication where یکی (a certain/one) precedes دخترست — an archaic contraction of دختر است (“is a daughter”).
segments.s058-g50: که is a relative pronoun (= “whose/that”) linking to the daughter; روی + ش is face + possessive enclitic (“her face”). ز is the poetic preposition meaning “from/than” used in comparative constructions. خورشید is the comparison standard “the sun,” and روشن‌ترست is a comparative adjective (روشن‌تر) with the older clitic copula -ست (= است), so “is brighter.”
segments.s058-g51: ز ... تا marks a span: ز سر تا به پایش = “from head to her foot.” Note the sequence: ز (from) + سر (head) + تا (to/until) + به پایش (to her foot) where پای + -ش is the possessed noun. به کردار عاج is a prepositional phrase “in the manner/form (کردار) of ivory (عاج),” with کردار functioning as “form/manner.”
segments.s058-g52: This is a series of comparative verbless clauses linked to the prior description: به رخ = “as to her face,” چون = “like,” بهشت = “paradise.” The copula is omitted (typical in Persian poetry). و connects the next clause: به بالا = “at the upper part/forehead,” چو (like/as) + ساج (the saj tree) completes the simile.
segments.s058-g53: بر آن = “upon that” (بر + demonstrative آن) pointing back to the previously described spot; سفت is an adjective “tight/firm” modifying the following noun. سیمنش is سیمن + enclitic -ش, literally “her silver” (i.e., a silver ornament belonging to/on her). مشکین modifies کمند — here two adjectives (سفت, مشکین) qualify کمند, which is a noun meaning “loop/noose/coil (ornamental band).”
segments.s058-g54: سرش = سر + enclitic -ش (“her head”). گشته is the perfect participle/compound verb meaning “has become/turned (into).” چون introduces a simile “like,” حلقه = “ring/loop,” and پای‑بند is a compound noun/adjective literally meaning “foot‑bound” (here physical/tightly bound), so the clause reads “her head has become like a ring bound to the foot.”
segments.s058-g55: رخانش is رخان + enclitic -ش, where رخان (here adjectival/nominal) describes a relaxed/soft aspect of the face (so “her soft/relaxed cheeks/appearance”). چو گلنار is a simile “like pomegranate blossom.” و connects the next nominal phrase لب ناردان = “lip(s) like nard” (ناردان = spikenard used as a simile). The copula is again omitted, a common poetic device.
segments.s058-g56: ز introduces origin (“from”); برش here functions as a spatial/relational element (literally ‘on it’ / ‘upon her’ in context) — the printed lemma also can mean ‘section/profile’ but here it picks up the idea “on/over her.” رسته is the perfect participle “has grown/sprung,” and ناروان is the noun that follows (the passage names ‘two ناروان’ having sprung). The phrase literally says “from (the) silver on her have sprung two ناروان.”
segments.s058-g81: بپرسید is an honorific/plural imperative 'ask' (literally 'inquire!'). کز من چه خواهی is a relative/from-phrase 'what you want from me' where کز = 'from' + pronoun من; خواهی is the 2nd-person present/expressive 'you want'. The final بخواه is an imperative 'ask (for it)' that refers back to 'چه' (i.e., 'ask for whatever you want').
segments.s058-g82: The repeated ز = 'from' (poetic form of از) introduces an enumerated list of sources/objects: تخت 'throne', مهر 'favor/kindness' (meaning honor/favour here), تیغ 'sword', and کلاه 'hat' (here 'helmet'). و simply links the items in the list.
segments.s058-g83: بدو گفت is inverted word order meaning 'to him he said' (بدو = 'to him' or 'unto him'); مهراب is the speaker's name; کای is the vocative 'O' addressing the king (an archaic/formal interjection), followed by پادشا 'king'. Note that classical Persian often places the indirect object before گفت.
segments.s058-g84: A string of epithets: سرافراز 'honoured/lofty' و پیروز 'and victorious' و فرمان روا is a two-word epithet where فرمان 'command' + روا (here functioning adjectivally) together mean 'one who rules by command' — i.e. 'sovereign/ruler'. Adjectives follow the noun they modify or appear in a list as here.
segments.s058-g85: مرا آرزو در زمانه یکیست literally reorders English as 'for me a desire in the age is one' — مرا (م + را here is the object/topic marker 'for me' / 'to me'), آرزو 'desire' is the theme, در زمانه 'in the time/age' is a locative phrase, and یکیست ('is one') is the copular predicate at the end. Classical Persian often places the possessor/topic (مرا) before the noun.
segments.s058-g86: که is a relative 'which/that' introducing a clause about 'آن آرزو' ('that desire'); بر تو دشوار نیست is a standard predicate 'is not difficult for you' where the prepositional phrase بر تو ('for you') precedes the adjective دشوار and the negative copula نیست (is not) closes the clause.
segments.s058-g87: که آیی is a purposive/relative clause 'that you come' (آیی is present/subjunctive/optative 'you come' — a hoped-for arrival). به شادی is an adverbial phrase 'with joy/joyfully', سوی خان من 'toward my khan/my court' (سوی = toward; خان here means 'court' or 'lord's hall'). Word order places the verb early in the clause in this poetic register.
segments.s058-g88: چو = 'as/like' introduces a simile; خورشید روشن کنی is a verb phrase where روشن (bright) is object/predicative and کنی (subjunctive of کردن) 'you make' follows — 'you make (me) bright like the sun'. جان من 'my life/soul' is the object with the possessor following the noun (standard Persian order: noun + possessor).
segments.s058-g33: پذیره شدش is a compact past construction: پذیره (reception/acceptance) + شد (past) with the enclitic ـش = “him,” so the sense is “he was received” (i.e. Zal received him). زال placed after the verb names the agent. The conjunction و links to بنواختش, another past verb with the same object enclitic meaning “(he) played/struck (for) him.”
segments.s058-g34: به آیین is a prepositional phrase meaning “in/according to the custom” (به used adverbially). یکی پایگه is the direct object phrase “a single/one station/post,” and ساختش is the past verb ساخت + enclitic ـش = “made/set him (in that post).” Word order is P‑phrase + object + verb with enclitic object.
segments.s058-g35: سوی تخت پیروزه is a prepositional phrase “toward the (turquoise) throne” (سوی + noun phrase). باز آمدند is the past plural “they came/returned,” with باز as an adverb meaning “again/returned.” The verb آمدند is plural past.
segments.s058-g36: گشاده دل is an adjectival phrase “open‑hearted,” followed by the coordinator و and the compound بزم ساز “feast‑maker / musician.” آمدند (past plural) applies to the whole coordinated description: “they came open‑hearted and as makers of the feast.”
segments.s058-g37: یکی پهلوانی نهادند is a verbal phrase “they set/established one feat of heroism” (یکی = “one/a,” پهلوانی = “heroic feat/contest,” نهادند = past plural ‘placed/established’). The final خوان here names the banquet context (poetically placed with the verb).
segments.s058-g38: نشستند is the past plural “they sat down” (here the sense is ‘sit’—the supplied lexicon entry was mismatched, so treat as the verb ‘to sit’). بر خوان is the locative “at/on the table,” and با فرخان marks accompaniment “with Farḵān.” Word order is verb + locative + accompaniment.
segments.s058-g39: گسارنده names the agent (here the cupbearer/pourer) followed by می‌می‌آورد, a narrative present/past sense “brought/was bringing” (in epic Persian such presentive forms narrate action), and و جام links the object “and the cup.” The supplied lexicon sense for گسارنده does not match the role here.
segments.s058-g40: نگه کرد is a light‑noun + verb idiom: نگه (a look/gaze) + کرد (“made/did”), meaning “cast a look” or “looked.” مهراب را is the definite object marked by را, and پور سام (literally “son of Sām”) is the agent/subject placed after the object for emphasis: “Sam’s son looked at Mehrab.”
segments.s058-g65: شب آمد is a temporal clause ('night came' = 'night fell'). پر اندیشه is an adjectival phrase 'full of thought' placed before the verb; بنشست is the past tense verb 'sat down' (archaic/poetic past form) and زال, the proper name, appears postverbally as the subject (poetic word order: Circumstance — predicate — subject).
segments.s058-g66: به + adjective + برگشت is an adverbial/verb cluster meaning 'he turned/withdrew in/into [that manner]' (here 'turned away unseen'); نادیده is an adjective 'unseen/ignored'. بی‌خورد و هال lists Zal's condition—'unfed (بی‌خورد) and in that state/condition (هال)'—with و simply coordinating the two nouns/adjectives; برگشت governs that whole descriptive remark.
segments.s058-g67: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; زد is the simple past 'struck' with its subject omitted (typical in epic style). بر سر کوه is a prepositional phrase 'upon the mountain's summit' (بر + سر + کوه). The second بر introduces the instrument or locus ('upon/with') and تیغ شید is a poetic image 'the blade of Shid' (تیغ used metaphorically as a ray/blade), so the whole phrase describes a striking/light image.
segments.s058-g68: Again چو introduces the temporal clause 'when'; یاقوت شد is 'became a ruby' (شد = become). روی 'face' refers poetically to the surface/face of something (here tied to the following گیتی), so روی گیتی سپید reads as 'the face of the world (گیتی) grew bright/white'—a compact poetic fold where colors/metaphors overlap (ruby/red and bright/white used for dawn imagery).
segments.s058-g69: در بار is a locative phrase 'in the (royal) court/at the hall' (در = 'in'); بگشاد is an older/poetic past form meaning 'opened' or 'spread' (here used as the verb); دستان سام is a genitival/possessive group 'the hands of Sam' functioning as the direct object—note the object appearing after the verb without the modern را because of poetic syntax.
segments.s058-g70: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/they departed' (subject is گردان 'troop', which here follows the verb as is common in epic word order). به زرین نیام is a prepositional phrase 'with (their) golden scabbards' (به marking accompaniment/association; زرین = 'golden', نیام = 'scabbard').
segments.s058-g71: در introduces the location 'in/at' (here 'in the hall/court'); پهلوان را uses the object marker را to mark 'the champion' as definite/direct object; بیاراستند is the past plural 'they adorned/arrayed' (poetic compound form built on آراستن), so the clause means 'they adorned the champion in the hall.'
segments.s058-g72: چو is again the temporal/conditional 'when/as'; بالای is 'upon/at the top of' or 'on high' and here indicates the target/position connected to the following noun; پرمایگان 'the nobles/great ones' is plural and خواستند is the past plural 'they desired/sought'—so the clause reads 'when the nobles wished (to go up/assume the high place)', with the verb in the plural agreeing with پرمایگان.
segments.s058-g89: چنین داد پاسخ is literal word order (چنین "thus" + داد "gave" + پاسخ "answer") meaning "he gave such an answer." که introduces the reported clause/quotation that follows. این رای نیست is an elliptical negative copula clause: این ("this") + رای (here = "counsel/plan") + نیست (present negative "is not"), i.e. "this counsel is not (acceptable/it will not do)."
segments.s058-g90: This is an archaic/poetic word order: به خان تو اندر مرا جای نیست literally arranges preposition + possessor + locative + object + noun + negation. اندر = "in/within," مرا (me) + را (object/topic marker) = "for me," جای = "place/room/standing," and نیست negates the whole clause. The whole phrase means "there is no place for me in/at your khan/court."
segments.s058-g91: نباشد here is a literary negative jussive/subjunctive ("may it not be" / "should it not be") used to set up a conditional; بدین corresponds to بدین/به‌دین "with this / to this" in archaic spelling (split here as ب + دین). همداستان means "of the same mind / in agreement." Altogether this segment expresses "if Sam is not of this mind/if Sam does not agree with this."
segments.s058-g92: همان شاه = "that/aforementioned king" (همان = that/the same, شاه = king). چون + بشنود introduces a temporal clause: چون here functions as "when/once" (classical usage), and بشنود is the present/subjunctive form of شنیدن "to hear" used for "when he hears." داستان = "the tale/story." The segment means "when that king hears the tale."
segments.s058-g93: که introduces the content clause ("that/that we..."). ما is the subject "we." می‌گساریم is a poetic present/future-ish form (prefix می- + verb) meaning to revel/spend time drinking or more generally to make merry (here translated "make merry"); verb-final شویم (from شدن) with مستان as predicate (مستان "drunkards"/"intoxicated") yields مستان شویم = "we become drunk." The conjunction و links the two actions.
segments.s058-g94: سوی = "toward/to," خانه = "house," بت پرستان is a compound: بت ("idol") + پرستان ("worshippers") = "idol-worshippers." شویم again is the 1pl present/subjunctive of شدن and here with سوی ... شویم means "we will go toward / we will proceed to" that place. Together: "we will go toward the house of the idol-worshippers."
segments.s058-g95: جز آن = "except for that / aside from that." هر چه گویی تو is a relative/subordinate clause meaning "whatever you say" (هر چه = "whatever," گویی here marks the speech/action "you say," with the pronoun تو). پاسخ دهم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative of دادن used as "I will give/offer a reply" (literally "I give answer" = "I will answer"). The clause means "except for that, whatever you say I will answer."
segments.s058-g96: به دیدار تو = literally "at the meeting/sight of you" i.e. "in your presence / when I see you." رای فرخ نهم: رای = "counsel/plan," فرخ = "fortunate/blessed," and نَهَم (here written نهم) is 1sg present/subjunctive of نهادن/دادن meaning "I will give/offer." So the phrase means "I will offer fortunate counsel in your presence."
segments.s058-g17: دل بخردان داشت is a compact possessive clause: دل (the possessed noun) followed by بخردان in a genitive/attributive relation (“of the wise”), then the past-tense verb داشت ‘had’ (3rd person). In Persian poetry the possessed noun normally appears before its modifier and the past verb can stand early, so this reads literally “(he) had the heart(s) of the wise.” مغز begins a second coordinated noun phrase that is governed by the same past verb (i.e. “and (he had) the brains...” ).
segments.s058-g18: This is a continuation of the list of things he possessed; the numeral phrase دو کتف یلان literally places the numeral دو before the noun کتف and the noun’s modifier یلان follows in the genitive/attributive position (“two shoulders of the heroes”). و links a final noun phrase هش موبدان. In classical/poetic Persian numerals and nouns may appear in this order; موبدان is the plural of موبد (Zoroastrian priests). The element هش here is archaic/uncertain in form (see note on senses).
segments.s058-g19: ز ضحاک introduces the source/agent with the poetic preposition ز ‘from’ followed by the proper name ضحاک (Zahhāk). تازی modifies گهر (tāzī-geharr = 'Arabian gem'); the adjective precedes the noun. داشتی is the 2nd-person past form (‘you had’/‘you possessed’) used here to report possession in past narration.
segments.s058-g20: به کابل is a locative/directional prepositional phrase (‘to/into Kabul’), then همه بوم is a quantified noun phrase with همه ‘all, the whole’ modifying بوم ‘land/realm’. و برداشتی continues the narrative with برداشتی as a past 2nd-person verb (‘you took/seized’); in poetic register برداشتن can mean to remove or to seize territory. Word order places the place phrase before the verb, as common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s058-g21: همی داد is the habitual/imperfective past construction (همی + past verb) meaning ‘would give/used to give’. هر سال is an adverbial time phrase ‘every year’ that follows the verb here (poetic word order). مر is the indirect object ‘to me’; سام ساو is the agent/subject (personal name(s)) placed after the object, a common flexible order in verse.
segments.s058-g22: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that'), با او به رزمش = ‘in combat with him’ (با او ‘with him’, به رزمش lit. ‘into/at his battle’). نبود is the past negative ‘was not/there was not’. ایچ is an emphatic negative ‘nothing/none’, so the clause states that there was nothing or no one comparable in battle toward him. تاو is uncertain/poetic here (see senses); the clause functions to justify the previous giving.
segments.s058-g23: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. آگه شد is a periphrastic past ‘became aware/learned’; this verb phrase is followed by an object introduced by از (about/of). کار دستان is a noun phrase ‘the affair/deeds of Dastān’ (کار = deed/affair; دستان here functions as the possessive/agent noun), and سام at the end is the personal name (subject) — in poetry the subject often appears after such clauses.
segments.s058-g24: ز کابل is the source prepositional phrase ‘from Kabul’ placed before the verb. بیامد is the simple past verb ‘came’. به هنگام is a fixed temporal prepositional phrase ‘at the time of…’ and بام (literally ‘roof/summit’ in the lexicon) is used poetically here as a time-word (i.e. at dawn / break of day). Together they form an adverbial of time attached to the coming.

sentences.s058-s57: Zal's heart suddenly went mad; wisdom departed, and love became sagacious.
sentences.s058-s58: The Arab commander, the upright chief, tells (relates) one tale about this.
sentences.s058-s59: For as long as I live, a leathern coat is my proper companion; the bend of the revolving wheel has kept (them) hidden as is fitting.
sentences.s058-s60: My bride — I must not be (made) fair, lest I be disgraced before the wise.
sentences.s058-s29: Her two eyes were like two narcissi in the garden; her eyelashes had taken a darkness from a crow's feather.
sentences.s058-s30: Her two eyebrows, like a finely fashioned bow; upon them a toz is laid, scented with musk and full of coquettish charm.
sentences.s058-s31: She is paradisal, wholly adorned throughout, filled with ornament, repose, and desire.
sentences.s058-s32: Zal's heart, for me, boiled with passion; it became such that from it calm and awareness departed.
sentences.s058-s5: Generous with treasure, and having cast suffering upon the rites and customs of the house Sepanj.
sentences.s058-s6: At that time he reached Kabul from Zābul — (they were) boars/grazing folk, laughing, and with joyful hearts.
sentences.s058-s7: There was a certain king named Mehrāb, mighty in power, with treasure and ample satisfaction.
sentences.s058-s8: Lofty above, in bearing a noble cypress; in countenance like the spring, and in his walk he scattered (all).
sentences.s058-s1: So ill (was it) that one day he formed such a plan that the kingship would be shaken from its place.
sentences.s058-s2: He went out with his elite corps; for with him their counsel and creed were one.
sentences.s058-s3: He decided to march toward the land of the Hindus — toward Kabul and Denbar and Margh and May.
sentences.s058-s4: He adorned every station with wine, music (instruments), and entertainers as he desired.
sentences.s058-s61: Zal became heart‑weary because of the wise men; he set his heart continually upon that matter.
sentences.s058-s62: His heart kept twisting with the conversation, for fear that this honour might be darkened.
sentences.s058-s63: For a while his heart would turn toward the vault of heaven; Zal's heart was wholly filled with love.
sentences.s058-s37: Mehrāb, the lord of Kābul, came out and went toward Zāl's tent — the lord of Zābul.
sentences.s058-s38: When he came near the royal court, a cry came from the gate: “Open the way!”
sentences.s058-s39: He entered into the hero's presence, saying, like a tree laden with fresh fruit.
sentences.s058-s40: Zāl's heart grew glad, and he began to play; from that assembly he lifted up his head.
sentences.s058-s21: The sight of her pleased him at once; his heart grew keener in his affair with her.
sentences.s058-s22: When Mehrab rose from the banquet, Zal looked and fixed his gaze on that lofty crest and mane.
sentences.s058-s23: Zal thus said to the chiefs: 'Gold is more fitting than a saddle with which to fasten the belt.'
sentences.s058-s24: A renowned man from among the nobles thus said, 'O champion of the world!'
sentences.s058-s13: Arrayed with treasure and steeds, (and) furnished with servants and every sort he desired.
sentences.s058-s14: With dinars and rubies, musk and fragrance; with brocade woven with gold and Chinese silk.
sentences.s058-s15: One crown with a regal gem, one golden collar set with peridot.
sentences.s058-s16: When the news came into Sam's hands that Mahrab had come to this noble (place/one).
sentences.s058-s53: No one cast an eye on him; they took him to be among the madmen.
sentences.s058-s54: When they saw the clear‑hearted champion, they found him so fervent in conversation.
sentences.s058-s55: The guests, one by one, praised him; the very one who had been hidden behind the curtain.
sentences.s058-s56: Because of his loftiness and his appearance and his gentleness; also because of his propriety and his worthiness.
sentences.s058-s49: When Mehrab heard, he cried "Afarin," and he instilled the impure religion into Zal's heart.
sentences.s058-s50: He strode proudly away from his throne, continually crying "Afarin" over his fortune.
sentences.s058-s51: When Sam's hands looked after him, they praised him abundantly, just as he deserved.
sentences.s058-s52: For that one for whom there was neither religion nor way, speech was too short to praise him.
sentences.s058-s25: Then, behind her veil there is a daughter whose face is brighter than the sun.
sentences.s058-s26: From head to foot she is, in appearance, like ivory; her face like paradise, and her brow like the saj‑tree.
sentences.s058-s27: Upon that is a tight silver, a musk‑dark loop; her head has become like a ring bound to the foot.
sentences.s058-s28: Her cheeks (or countenance) are like the pomegranate blossom and her lips like nard; from the silver on her have sprung two narvān.
sentences.s058-s41: Ask (him): whatever you want from me, ask — from throne and from favor and from sword and helmet.
sentences.s058-s42: To him Mehrāb said: 'O king, honoured and victorious and ruler by command...'
sentences.s058-s43: I have one desire in this age, which that desire is not hard for you.
sentences.s058-s44: That you come joyfully to my court, and (that) like the sun you brighten my life.
sentences.s058-s17: Zāl received him and struck up (a tune) for him, and in accordance with custom set him in a post/station.
sentences.s058-s18: They returned toward the turquoise throne, and came with open hearts and as makers of the feast (bringing music).
sentences.s058-s19: They staged a feat of heroism and sat down at the banquet‑table with Farḵān.
sentences.s058-s20: The cupbearer brought (it) and the cup was held — Sam’s son cast his gaze on Mehrab.
sentences.s058-s33: Night fell; Zal sat down full of thought and withdrew unseen, unfed and in that state.
sentences.s058-s34: When a blade struck the mountain's summit, the face of the world became like a ruby and grew bright.
sentences.s058-s35: In the court Sam spread his hands; the troop departed with golden scabbards.
sentences.s058-s36: They adorned the champion in the hall when the nobles wished to ascend.
sentences.s058-s45: He answered thus: "This counsel is not acceptable; there is no place for me in your court."
sentences.s058-s46: Should Sam not be of this mind, when that king hears the tale...
sentences.s058-s47: That we shall make merry and become drunk, and go toward the house of the idol-worshippers.
sentences.s058-s48: Aside from that, whatever you say I will answer; in your presence I will offer fortunate counsel.
sentences.s058-s9: He had the hearts of the wise and the brains of the elders; the two shoulders of the heroes and eight mobeds.
sentences.s058-s10: You had an Arabian gem from Zahhāk, and you seized all the territory of Kabul.
sentences.s058-s11: Each year Sam Saav used to give to me; for there was no one who could match him in battle.
sentences.s058-s12: When Sam became aware of Dastān’s deeds, he came from Kabul at the break of day.
